title: Before
content:
I.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let them fight it out, friend! things have gone too far.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God must judge the couple: leave them as they are
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whichever oneן¿½s the guiltless, to his glory,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And whichever one the guiltן¿½s with, to my story!

II.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, you would not bid men, sunk in such a slough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strike no arm out further, stick and stink as now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leaving right and wrong to settle the embroilment,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heaven with snaky hell, in torture and entoilment?

III.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whoן¿½s the culprit of them? How must he conceive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God, the queen he caps to, laughing in his sleeve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis but decent to profess oneself beneath her:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still, one must not be too much in earnest, either!

IV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Better sin the whole sin, sure that God observes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then go live his life out! Life will try his nerves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the sky, which noticed all, makes no disclosure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the earth keeps up her terrible composure.

V.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let him pace at pleasure, past the walls of rose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pluck their fruits when grape-trees graze him as he goes!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he ן¿½gins to guess the purpose of the garden,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the sly mute thing, beside there, for a warden.

VI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatן¿½s the leopard-dog-thing, constant at his side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A leer and lie in every eye of its obsequious hide?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When will come an end to all the mock obeisance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the price appear that pays for the misfeasance?

VII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So much for the culprit. Whoן¿½s the martyred man?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let him bear one stroke more, for be sure he can!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He that strove thus evilן¿½s lump with good to leaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let him give his blood at last and get his heaven!

VIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All or nothing, stake it! Trust she God or no?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus far and no farther? farther? be it so!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, enough of your chicane of prudent pauses,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sage provisos, sub-intents and saving-clauses!

IX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, ן¿½forgiveן¿½ you bid him? While Godן¿½s champion lives,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wrong shall be resisted: dead, why, he forgives.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But you must not end my friend ere you begin him;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Evil stands not crowned on earth, while breath is in him.

X.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Once more, Will the wronger, at this last of all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dare to say, ן¿½I did wrong,ן¿½ rising in his fall?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No? Let go then! Both the fighters to their places!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While I count three, step you back as many paces!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
